title: Code, Craft & Culture: Building from Rural Japan
description: What it's like developing software and building digital products from Okayama, Japan—away from the tech hubs.
content:
Code, Craft & Culture: Building from Rural Japan

Most developers dream of working from San Francisco, New York, or Tokyo. I chose rural Okayama, Japan—and it's one of the best decisions I've made.

Why Rural Japan?

After years working in Kyoto and Tokyo, I moved to Okayama Prefecture in 2019. Here's what drew me:

  • Lower cost of living: More space, less stress
  • Rich culture: Traditional crafts, festivals, local artisans
  • Natural beauty: Mountains, coastline (Ushimado), countryside
  • Calm pace: Time to think, create, and practice Tai-Chi (太極拳)

The Remote Work Reality

Working remotely from rural Japan has unique challenges and advantages:

Challenges

  • Time zones: Client calls at odd hours (SF = 17 hours behind)
  • Language barriers: Not all services speak English
  • Limited tech events: No meetups or conferences nearby
  • Slow internet (sometimes): Rural areas can have connectivity issues

Advantages

  • Deep focus: Fewer distractions, more productive hours
  • Lower expenses: More runway for experiments and side projects
  • Cultural immersion: Daily interaction with Japanese culture
  • Quality of life: Fresh air, nature, slower rhythm

What I'm Building

Living here has influenced what I build:

Bento Kumiko

A curated guide to restaurants, artisans, and stays across Japan. Born from my own explorations and recommendations I gave to visiting friends.

Ushimado Town

A directory for my local area—highlighting businesses and events in this small coastal town. Helping the community get online.

Travel Planning Services

Organizing team retreats and custom Japan experiences for remote teams who want to work from somewhere inspiring.

The Tai-Chi Connection (太極拳)

Practicing Tai-Chi with Master Kiyoko Kashiwagi has taught me patience, discipline, and focus—qualities that translate directly to software development.

The slow, deliberate movements mirror the process of writing clean code. The emphasis on balance reflects the need to balance work with life.

For Other Remote Builders

If you're considering working from rural Japan:

  1. Visit first: Spend a month to see if the lifestyle fits
  2. Learn basic Japanese: Google Translate only gets you so far
  3. Build community: Join local clubs, volunteer, connect
  4. Embrace the pace: Don't fight the slower rhythm—use it
  5. Stay connected: Regular video calls with collaborators

Conclusion

You don't need to be in a tech hub to build great products. Sometimes the best work happens away from the noise, in places that give you space to think and create.

Rural Japan offers that space—plus incredible culture, food, and natural beauty.

If you're curious about working or visiting rural Japan, or organizing a team retreat here, let's talk.

Photos and stories from Okayama coming soon. Subscribe to the newsletter (below) to stay updated.

title: Building a Minimal Blog with Vite and TypeScript
description: How I built a zero-maintenance blog system using markdown, static generation, and vanilla JavaScript—no frameworks required.
content:
Building a Minimal Blog with Vite and TypeScript

When building my portfolio site, I wanted to add a blog but had one strict requirement: write once, no maintenance, stable for years.

No frameworks that change every six months. No complicated build pipelines. No databases. Just markdown files that get converted to static HTML at build time.

The Stack

Here's what I chose:

  • Vite - Already using it for the portfolio
  • TypeScript - Type safety for the build script
  • gray-matter - Parse YAML frontmatter
  • marked - Convert markdown to HTML
  • Vanilla JavaScript - No React, no Vue, no framework

That's it. Four dependencies total.

How It Works

1. Write Posts in Markdown

Each post is a simple markdown file in /posts:

---
title: "My Post Title"
date: 2024-12-10
description: "Short description"
tags: ["vite", "typescript"]
draft: false
---

Your content here...

2. Build Script Generates JSON

A Node script reads all markdown files, parses them, and outputs a single JSON file:

// scripts/build-posts.ts
import fs from 'fs';
import matter from 'gray-matter';
import { marked } from 'marked';

// Read markdown files
// Parse frontmatter
// Convert to HTML
// Output to src/data/posts.json

3. Pages Read from JSON

The blog index and individual post pages simply import the JSON file:

import posts from './src/data/posts.json';

// Render posts

Why This Approach?

Advantages:

  • ✅ No runtime dependencies (everything is static)
  • ✅ Fast builds (just parsing markdown)
  • ✅ Version controlled (posts are in git)
  • ✅ Portable (markdown works everywhere)
  • ✅ Future-proof (no framework lock-in)

Trade-offs:

  • ❌ No dynamic features (comments, likes, etc.)
  • ❌ Need to rebuild to publish
  • ❌ All posts loaded at once (fine for small blogs)

For my use case—occasional technical posts with no dynamic features—this is perfect.

SEO Considerations

Since this is a static site, I used:

  • Query parameters for post URLs (/post.html?slug=my-post)
  • Vercel rewrites for clean URLs (/blog/my-post)
  • Dynamic meta tags set via JavaScript after page load

Not perfect for SEO, but good enough for a personal blog.

Next Steps

I'm planning to add:

  • RSS feed generation
  • Newsletter integration with Resend
  • Sitemap generation
  • Code syntax highlighting

All of these can be done at build time, keeping the runtime completely static.

Conclusion

You don't need a framework to build a blog. Sometimes the simplest solution—markdown files and a build script—is all you need.

The code for this blog system is on GitHub. Feel free to use it as a starting point for your own minimal blog.

Have questions about this setup? Reach out and let's chat.

title: Welcome to My Blog
description: Introducing my new blog where I'll share thoughts on tech, Japan, and building digital products from rural Okayama.
content:
Welcome to My Blog

I'm excited to launch this new space where I'll be sharing my journey as a builder, developer, and explorer based in rural Japan.

What You'll Find Here

This blog is where I'll document:

  • Tech experiments: AI, blockchain, web development, and other technical explorations
  • Life in Japan: Insights from living and working in Okayama, cultural observations, and local discoveries
  • Building digital products: Lessons learned from creating Bento Kumiko, Ushimado Town, and other projects
  • Tai-Chi practice (太極拳): Reflections on martial arts training and discipline

Why Now?

After years of building products and helping others tell their stories, it's time to share my own. This blog represents a commitment to documenting what I'm learning, creating, and discovering.

What's Next?

I'm working on several exciting projects and have plenty of ideas to explore. Stay tuned for posts about:

  • Setting up a minimal blog system with Vite and TypeScript
  • Travel planning strategies for Japan
  • Code experiments and technical deep-dives
  • Team-building retreats in rural Japan

Thanks for reading, and welcome aboard!

Want to chat? Get in touch or find me on LinkedIn.

title: Your Complete Guide to Master DevOps
description: A curated collection of YouTube tutorials covering DevOps fundamentals to advanced topics like Docker, Terraform, Kubernetes, and Ansible.
content:
I created this collection from YouTube tutorials for those looking to learn DevOps, from the fundamentals to advanced topics like Docker, Terraform, and Ansible.

Whether you're a beginner or looking to expand your knowledge, these links offer valuable insights and practical knowledge to help you grow and advance in the field.

DevOps Workflow Overview

Here is a general DevOps workflow:

  1. Development: Code is written and tested by developers. They use tools such as Git for version control and IDEs such as Visual Studio Code for development.

  2. Continuous Integration: Developers push their code to a version control system like Git, where a Continuous Integration (CI) tool like Jenkins or Travis CI takes over. The CI tool compiles the code and runs automated tests to catch any issues early.

  3. Continuous Deployment: Once the code is tested, it is deployed to a staging environment for further testing. If the tests pass, the code is then deployed to a production environment.

  4. Monitoring: After deployment, the production environment is monitored for performance and errors. Tools like Nagios, Datadog, or New Relic can be used for monitoring.

  5. Continuous Feedback: Feedback from monitoring is used to improve the application and the DevOps process.

Popular DevOps Tools

  • Git - for version control
  • Jenkins or Travis CI - for Continuous Integration
  • Ansible or Puppet - for configuration management
  • Docker or Kubernetes - for containerization
  • Nagios, Datadog, or New Relic - for monitoring

This is a high-level overview of a DevOps workflow. The exact tools and processes can vary depending on the organization's needs.

DevOps Pre-requisite

  • The (Short) History of DevOps
  • DevOps Prerequisites Course - Getting started with DevOps

Networking

  • DevOps - Networking knowledge base

  • Linux Tutorial for Beginners: Introduction to Linux Operating System

  • Git & GitHub Crash Course For Beginners

  • GUI Tools - List for all platforms

Databases

  • SQL Tutorial - Full Database Course for Beginners
  • MySQL Tutorial for Beginners - Full Course

Artifact Repository Manager

  • Nexus Repository | DevOps Tool | Intellipaat

Docker

  • Docker Tutorial for Beginners

Jenkins

  • Jenkins Full Course in 4 Hours | Edureka

AWS

  • AWS Tutorial For Beginners | AWS Full Course | Edureka
  • AWS Certified Solutions Architect - Associate 2020

SSH

MobaXterm

  • How To SSH into EC2 Instance Using MobaXterm

PuTTY

  • How to Install PuTTY on Windows + SSH Connections

  • YAML Tutorial for Beginners: Learn YAML in-depth for DevOps

Kubernetes

  • Terraform Course - Automate your AWS cloud infrastructure

Python

Grafana

  • Grafana Tutorial For Beginners | Edureka
  • Grafana and Prometheus Crash Course

title: Web 3.0 Fundamentals | From 0 to 1
description: A comprehensive weekly newsletter series covering Web3 fundamentals including wallets, DeFi, NFTs, bridges, stablecoins, and more.
content:
This was originally a weekly newsletter series that I kept updating throughout 2023. I've compiled all the lessons here as a comprehensive guide to Web3 fundamentals.

Summer Web 3.0 Q&A Sessions

After the newsletter series, I offered one-to-one Q&A sessions for questions related to all the topics covered. If you're interested in learning more about Web3, feel free to reach out.

Week of May 9, 2023

Account Abstraction

  • WTF is Account Abstraction
  • Technical Documentation
  • Application - Avocado

Week of April 18, 2023

Web 3.0 Data Analytics

  • Guide to Web3 Data
  • Beginner's Guide to Ethereum and SQL
  • Become a Data Wizard

DeFi

  • Provide Liquidity and Earn Interest
  • Borrow Assets
  • Bonus: Get an early CoinBase Builder NFT

Week of April 11, 2023

  • Intro to DeFi - Earn a free NFT badge
  • Beige Paper | Simplified Ethereum Whitepaper
  • How does Ethereum work, anyway?

Week of April 3, 2023

What is a Stablecoin?

Please read these articles to get a good understanding of the stablecoin concept:

  • Digital money for everyday use
  • Stablecoin Overview

The first stablecoin, DAI, was developed by MakerDAO. Since then, many stablecoins have been released. Below are the most used stablecoins:

  • USD Coin (USDC) - Always-on dollars, internet speed
  • USDT - Tether Token
  • DAI - A better, smarter currency

Week of March 28, 2023

What is a Bridge?

A bridge is a connection that allows two different blockchain networks to communicate with each other and exchange assets.

You can think of each blockchain as an island, with its own rules, money, language, and so on. A crypto bridge is the term for the networked route connecting these two islands.

In a previous week (Feb 27, 2023), we talked about gas fees. If you have interacted with the Ethereum Mainnet network (Layer 1), you have probably noticed that gas fees can be very expensive.

Learn more:

  • Layer 1
  • Layer 2

Bridges facilitate communication between Layer 1 and Layer 2.

Resources:

  • Bridge configuration for wallet
  • List of bridges and interoperability

Week of March 14, 2023

Platforms to Practice

The best way to learn, explore, and succeed in Web3:

  • Layer3 - Get started with Quests
  • Galxe - Create Impactful Experiences With Web3 Credentials

Register on both platforms. Once done, you can start completing quests to earn rewards while learning.

Week of March 7, 2023

Community Update

It's been already 4 weeks! I hope you enjoyed it and learned something.

For this week, there was no new material—instead, I created a community group on Discord for discussions and exchanges.

  • Join Discord

Week of Feb 27, 2023

Gas and Fees

  • Gas and fee explained
  • What is gas and why do transactions take so long

Transactions

  • What is a blockchain transaction
  • Visualizer of live transactions on Ethereum and Bitcoin

Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs)

  • What is an NFT

Week of Feb 20, 2023

What is DeFi?

DeFi refers to a new financial system that operates on a decentralized, blockchain-based infrastructure, rather than relying on traditional financial intermediaries like banks and exchanges.

DeFi aims to provide open, transparent, and permissionless financial services to anyone with an internet connection.

Action for this Week:

Please watch this video series: DeFi 101 - What is DeFi?

Week of Feb 13, 2023

What is a Cryptocurrency Wallet?

For this week, everyone should have a crypto wallet (desktop). This step is very important.

Action for this Week:

  1. Download the Brave web browser (not mandatory but highly recommended)
  2. Go to MetaMask to create your wallet (your passport to interact with the blockchain)

Understanding Wallets

To simplify, let me use the analogy of the internet. In Web2.0, to interact with any application (Instagram, Facebook, Gmail, online bank, etc.), you need an email address. This email address gives you access to the application.

  • If you LOSE your password, you can no longer access your email or the application.
  • If someone STEALS your password, that person can access your email or banking account.

The power of a wallet is that it gives you complete control of everything you do on the blockchain.

WARNING: You must write down and store your private key in a safe place. If you lose it, you'll lose everything associated with that address.

More detailed definition: What is a crypto wallet

Thank you for reading. If you found this post valuable and would like to receive more information like this, please consider subscribing to my newsletter. I regularly share updates on new technologies, blockchain, AI, and other topics that may be of interest to you.

title: Step-by-Step Guide to Becoming a Software Developer: Learn to Code and Get Hired!
description: A comprehensive guide with free resources to learn coding, build your portfolio, and land your first software development job.
content:
I am always surprised by the number of people who are still not aware of available resources to learn almost anything for free. From Web Developer, Artificial Intelligence, Augmented Reality, Blockchain, Data scientist, etc. The contents are of very good quality and some of them are proposed by prestigious universities such as Harvard and Stanford. There are also different types of online help, mentorship, and forums for groups taking the same class, to ask questions, share tips, etc.

Besides that, there are resources dedicated to helping start a new career as a software engineer. The resources have information on creating an online profile, preparing for interviews, doing game-coding, and sharing personal work on a platform like GitHub where recruiters can review.

During my trip to Rwanda, I did a few workshops to share resources that I hope would help newly graduated students to find a remote job. Below, I am sharing a list of links that offer some advice on how to land a remote job or start a new career as a software engineer.

1. Outsourcing Job | Remote Work Websites

How to Get More Interviews, More Job Offers, and Even a Raise:

  • Upwork
  • Indeed - search for remote jobs
  • Freelancer
  • Codeable - for WordPress developers
  • Outsource
  • Hired
  • We Work Remotely
  • Remotive Jobs

Helpful reads:

  • The Ultimate Guide to Remote Work
  • 24 of the Best Remote Jobs Boards

2. Education, Education, Education

My Recommendations

  1. First: Start learning HTML/CSS
  2. Second: Learn JavaScript for Front-End
  3. Last: Learn at least one language for Back-End (Python, Java, PHP, Ruby, etc.)

List of Free Online Classes

  • freeCodeCamp - Learn to code for free, get hired in 5 months, and have fun along the way
  • Codecademy - Learn to Code for Free
  • Learn CSS
  • Exercism - Code Practice and Mentorship for Everyone
  • FutureLearn
  • W3Schools - Online Web Tutorials
  • MDN Web Docs - Mozilla Developer Network
  • Udacity - Learn the Latest Tech Skills
  • Scrimba
  • CSS Tricks - Front-End
  • Treehouse - Start Learning for Free
  • Pluralsight - Code Courses
  • Coursera - Online Courses & Credentials by Top Educators
  • RubyMonk - Interactive Ruby tutorials
  • Khan Academy - Free Online Courses, Lessons & Practice
  • Android Developers - Developer Guides
  • OpenClassrooms - Fully-online programs and free courses
  • Le Wagon Workshops

Contributing to Open Source

Contributing to open source projects is very appreciated by recruiters. Look for guides on how to contribute to Open Source projects.

Online Communities for Help

  • DEV Community
  • Stack Overflow - Where Developers Learn, Share, & Build Careers
  • CodePen - Build, Test, and Discover Front-end Code

Web Development

I created a list of resources dedicated to website development that you can find on my Web Dev Resources page.

Learn How to Work on Collaborative Projects

  • Git and GitHub for Beginners
  • Oh My Git! - An open source game about learning Git!

3. Build Your Portfolio

Learn by doing and publish your work on GitHub or DEV Community to increase your visibility.

4. Prepare for Interviews

  • CodinGame
  • Coderbyte
  • CodeChef
  • HackerRank
  • LeetCode
  • Geektastic
  • Project Euler - Mathematical/computer programming challenges

Bonus Resources

  • La voie du Tao
  • Do one thing well
  • What is code?
  • Lectures on Digital Photography
  • MOOC Lists - Massive open online courses
  • State of JavaScript

Thank you for reading. If you found this post valuable and would like to receive more information like this, please consider subscribing to my newsletter. I regularly share updates on new technologies, blockchain, AI, and other topics that may be of interest to you.

title: Become a Web3 Developer with Ethereum Blockchain: A Guide to Learning and Getting Hired!
description: A curated collection of resources to help you become a blockchain developer, focusing on Ethereum, Solidity, and Web3 job opportunities.
content:
I wrote a post to share resources for starting a career in software engineering. Since then, I have developed an interest in blockchain and wrote a post encouraging investment in education instead of speculation.

For those who are new to software development, I recommend first learning to code. There are several resources available, which can be found in my software developer guide and DevOps guide.

Today, I would like to share some resources that can help you become a blockchain developer, with a focus on Ethereum blockchain, which has been my area of primary interest.

Table of Contents

  2. Advanced and Academic Materials

  4. Get Paid to Do Great Work

Advanced and Academic Materials

title: Privacy, Anonymity and Surveillance on the Blockchain
description: Exploring the challenges of privacy on public blockchains and the emerging solutions like Zero Knowledge Proofs and ring signatures.
content:
Originally published in October 2017. At that time, I was fascinated by the potential for the technology to bring privacy and data ownership. Even today, privacy remains a challenge for the blockchain as it tries to attract more users while balancing the need for privacy.

The blockchain is a decentralized, digital ledger that records transactions on multiple computers in a secure and transparent manner. It eliminates the need for intermediaries, allowing parties to make direct, secure transactions without the risk of tampering or fraud.

The Four Key Characteristics of Blockchain Technology

1. Cryptographic Security

All transactions are signed by a public/private key protecting them from fraud.

2. Decentralized

Blockchain is a decentralized peer-to-peer network where all transactions are replicated, which means that no single participant can tamper with the data. To update the data a majority consensus is required.

3. Immutable Ledger

A write-once database that records every transaction that occurs. An immutable history of every transaction.

4. Smart Contracts

Smart contracts are programmable contracts that secure, enforce and execute settlement of recorded agreement between people and organizations. (Definition proposed by Nick Szabo)

Types of Blockchain

There are several types of blockchain, including:

  • Public blockchain: Anyone can participate in the consensus of validating the chain and decide which one gets added to the blockchain. All transactions are public and anyone can read them. Examples include Bitcoin and Ethereum.

  • Consortium blockchain: The blockchain consensus is validated by a set of nodes. The right to read may be public or restricted.

  • Fully private blockchain: This is a centralized server controlled by one organization.

  • Hybrid blockchain: A combination of two or more blockchain networks to create a new, unified network.

  • Sidechain: A separate blockchain that operates alongside a main blockchain, providing additional functionality.

The Case for Public Blockchains

We will focus on public blockchains, which are open ledgers accessible to anyone and where all transactions can be viewed.

This transparency is good for:

  • Ensuring validity: All transactions are valid (e.g., people want to be able to see that their vote has been counted).

  • Transparency: All transactions are public and everyone can access the history to know how it got to the current state (e.g., for public service or government, anyone can see how the taxes they are paying are spent and fight corruption).

  • Immutability: The "Proof of Work" process used to validate each block makes it impossible to modify the state of the blockchain.

The Transparency Problem

The transparency can be used against users.

There are several cases where the transparency of blockchain could be used against people. For example, knowing someone's address, it becomes possible to track how they spend money and to whom they send money.

How About Fungibility?

Fungibility refers to the property of a commodity, currency or asset that makes it interchangeable with another unit of the same value. Fungibility is important in financial transactions, as it ensures that each unit of currency or asset has the same value, regardless of its history or origin.

With the transparency of an open ledger like a public blockchain, where the provenance of every coin can be traced, we can envision a scenario where individuals begin to reject money for various reasons. For example, if a coin has been traded on an illegal exchange platform, it may be refused. Similarly, one could imagine being refused credit at an organic shop if their bank is associated with deforestation in the Amazon.

How Can We Bring More Privacy for Users?

Different solutions are being developed to bring more privacy on the blockchain:

  • Private blockchain can ensure privacy for transactions if you trust the nodes in charge not to disclose the information—but in this case it is really difficult to guarantee the integrity of the process.

  • Mixers and CoinJoins offer solutions to privacy with the only problem that you must trust the middleman and hope that it never gets compromised or hacked by a third party.

  • Public blockchain ledger offers a solution if you build applications that make sure that private data doesn't get on the ledger or is stored in encrypted form.

One of the challenges is to guarantee the integrity of the process while ensuring privacy at the same time.

How to Encrypt Data and Prove It Satisfies Rules?

Linkable ring signature is one of the options being explored. The linkable ring signature is a cryptographic signature where the identity of the signer in a ring signature remains anonymous, but two ring signatures can be linked if they are signed by the same person.

For e-voting, linkable ring signature can be used to allow a person's vote without revealing their identity—but two votes from the same person will be invalid.

The Ethereum Metropolis (Byzantium) upgrade added a new cryptographic tool (zk-SNARKs) that can do linkable ring signature, and we are seeing new features to improve privacy.

The Future of Blockchain Privacy

Updated in 2023: Various solutions are being developed to enhance privacy on the blockchain. One such technology is Zero Knowledge Proof, which offers a secure way to verify information without revealing the actual data.

Thank you for reading. If you found this post valuable and would like to receive more information like this, please consider subscribing to my newsletter. I regularly share updates on new technologies, blockchain, AI, and other topics that may be of interest to you.
